record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: County Recorder (marriage licenses from 1958 statewide; earlier records at District Court)
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1900 (births and deaths statewide registration); marriage records kept at county level from territorial era

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Burned Counties Research wiki; Minnesota Historical Society county records guides
administrative_notes: Minnesota became a territory in 1849 and a state in 1858. Its 87 counties were organized between 1849 and 1922. Settlement patterns include early French-Canadian fur traders, Scandinavian immigration (1860s-1920s), German immigration, and significant Ojibwe and Dakota populations. The state has remarkably few burned counties compared to southern states. URLs in this file should be verified before production use.

core_repositories.description: Primary state historical society and archive. Houses state census records, county records, manuscripts, newspapers, photographs, and maps. Gale Family Library open for genealogy research. Collections include 500,000+ photographs, 40,000+ maps, and extensive manuscript collections.
Searchable online catalog of MNHS collections including manuscripts, photographs, maps, artifacts, and library materials. Not all items digitized but catalog records indicate availability.
Official state archives housed within MNHS. Holds state government records, territorial records, county records on microfilm, and vital records indexes.
Volunteer-maintained county-level genealogy pages with transcribed records, cemetery listings, and research guides for all 87 MN counties.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive MN microfilm and digitized records. Includes county-level deed, probate, marriage, naturalization, and court records. Many collections indexed and name-searchable.
Consolidated list of all online MN genealogy records available through FamilySearch.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / National Digital Newspaper Program
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized and searchable Minnesota newspaper titles. Free full-text search. Part of the National Digital Newspaper Program.
MNHS newspaper collection includes 7,000+ newspaper titles. Digital access to selected titles through the Minnesota Digital Newspaper Hub.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Minnesota was a free state, but African American history in MN dates to the fur trade era. MNHS holds records of early Black communities in St. Paul, Minneapolis, and Stillwater. Includes records of the state's anti-slavery societies and Underground Railroad connections.

Dred Scott lived at Fort Snelling (now Hennepin County) 1836-1840. His case originated partly from his residence in free territory (Minnesota/Wisconsin). Related records at National Archives and Missouri Historical Society.

native_american_and_removal_records.description: Minnesota has 11 federally recognized tribal nations: 7 Ojibwe (Anishinaabe) bands and 4 Dakota communities. MNHS holds extensive records including treaty documents, agency records, annuity rolls, and missionary records.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: National Archives — Kansas City and Washington
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Minnesota Ojibwe agencies (White Earth, Red Lake, Leech Lake, Fond du Lac, Grand Portage, Bois Forte, Mille Lacs). Includes allotment records, annuity rolls, census rolls, and school records. Records span 1820s-1960s.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: National Archives — Kansas City and Washington
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Bureau of Indian Affairs records for Minnesota Dakota agencies. Includes records related to the 1862 US-Dakota War, forced removal, and subsequent reservation communities (Upper Sioux, Lower Sioux, Prairie Island, Shakopee Mdewakanton). Many Dakota were forcibly relocated to Crow Creek, South Dakota after 1862.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Dawes Rolls and Allotment Records (Minnesota Ojibwe)
native_american_and_removal_records.description: Allotment records for White Earth, Red Lake, and other MN Ojibwe reservations under the Dawes Act and Nelson Act (1889). Critical for documenting tribal membership and land ownership. Note: Red Lake Band successfully resisted allotment and maintains unallotted reservation.

military_records.description: Minnesota sent 24,000+ soldiers to the Civil War (including the famous First Minnesota Infantry at Gettysburg). MNHS holds muster rolls, service records, and pension records. Also WWI, WWII, Korean War, and Vietnam records.

land_and_property.source_name: Minnesota Bureau of Land Management — Land Patents
land_and_property.repository: Bureau of Land Management / General Land Office
land_and_property.description: Federal land patents for Minnesota. Minnesota was a public domain state — original land titles came from federal government. BLM General Land Office Records has searchable database of all federal land patents.
Directory of all 87 MN county recorder offices. Many counties now offer online deed searches. Contact individual county offices for online portal access.

vital_records.source_name: Minnesota Vital Records — Department of Health
vital_records.description: Statewide birth and death registration began in 1900. Pre-1900 vital events found in county records, church records, and cemetery records. MNHS holds indexes to early vital records.

Minnesota conducted state censuses in addition to federal censuses. These are especially valuable since the 1890 federal census was largely destroyed. The 1857 territorial census and 1905 state census are particularly detailed. Available through MNHS and FamilySearch.

church_and_cemetery.description: Minnesota has extensive church records reflecting Scandinavian (Lutheran), German (Catholic/Lutheran), and Irish (Catholic) immigration. Norwegian, Swedish, and Finnish Lutheran church records are particularly valuable for immigrant research. Many microfilmed by FamilySearch. Concordia College (Moorhead) holds significant Norwegian-American church records.

immigration_and_naturalization.description: Minnesota's heavy immigrant population makes naturalization records critical. County district courts handled naturalizations. MNHS holds microfilm copies of many county naturalization indexes and records. Declarations of intention, petitions, and certificates available for most counties.

World-class collection documenting immigration to North America. Particularly strong in Scandinavian, Eastern European, and Finnish immigration records. Holds personal papers, organizational records, newspapers, and photographs from immigrant communities.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Maintains library, offers classes, publishes Minnesota Genealogist quarterly.

Research center focused on the Iron Range region (St. Louis, Itasca, and surrounding counties). Houses records from mining companies, immigrant communities (Finnish, Slovenian, Croatian, Italian, Serbian), and labor organizations. Essential for northeastern MN research.

historical_counties_extinct.description: Minnesota Territory was organized in 1849 with 9 original counties. By statehood in 1858, there were 57 counties. The final county (Lake of the Woods) was organized in 1922. Several extinct/renamed counties: Breckinridge County (renamed Clay in 1862 due to Civil War politics), Buchanan County (renamed Pine in 1862), Andy Johnson County (briefly proposed 1868, never organized), Doty County (renamed St. Louis in 1855), Pembina County (transferred to Dakota Territory 1861), Superior County (proposed, never organized). Researchers should check predecessor county names for early territorial records.

burned_counties_summary.authority: FamilySearch Burned Counties Research wiki; Minnesota Historical Society county records guides

counties.losses: Courthouse fire destroyed county records. The Hinckley Fire of 1894 devastated the region.
